speaker: interviewee
text: --family together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was a day before-- in the evening, a day before we knew that--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew through the sergeant. His name was Karolek. He was a good friend of ours. My sister used to go to her a lot to sell stuff. And she would give money.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he liked us a lot, because we had a story that when my father built the house, we had a very long lot. He built the house closer to the road. Then about a few years later-- I remember that story-- somebody else bought the lot next to us. And he built the house in the back, closer to the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we had our sheds, with a goat and chickens and everything, , in the back, with the outdoor houses, you know, the outhouses? So he didn't like it. So he kept taking us to court. And this sergeant was delivering the subpoena to my father that he should appear in court, because the guy, the neighbor, took us to court. But he always lost, because the judge always asks my father, now, who was first that built the house? It was my father. He says, case dismissed. Every time a new judge came, he took us to court again. And the same sergeant delivered the subpoena again. So I knew of him as well. But my older sisters knew him more than me. And he became like a friend of the family. So when my sister came to him to sell something, he says, by the way, I have to tell you that tomorrow the Germans will come. And they'll take everything, everybody out from the camp, from the ghetto. So when my sister said it, it's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father gathered the girls, because the boy, he-- my father knew he would not go on his own. And he said that Sarah and Naomi should go together. Me, he gave to the court reporter, because he also became our friend. Ludwiczek was his name. And my oldest, Rachel, that was oldest from the bunch that wasn't married yet, she went to this Karolek, to the sergeant.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we knew where everybody is going. Well, Sarah and Naomi were supposed-- they were supposed to go to the house, where we had our house, to the Polish people that we knew. And I was supposed to go to this Ludwiczek. And my sister went to Karolek. And so my sister got a diamond ring, a beautiful diamond ring from my brother to give to this Karolek for keeping her. Now, I got some merchandise from my sister's store. And so did the other two sisters. And at night, we would-- as I said, there were no Germans standing near the-- near the fences, so we could go over the fences very easily. And each one went to-- to where they were supposed to go. My sister Rachel, she said, we're not going unless you go with us, to my father and mother and brother. And he said, I lived to do the commandments. And I did them already. But you girls did not have a chance, so you have to survive, just like it's written in the Torah, [SPEAKING HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have to get married and have a family on your own. And we are only going to hamper you in your survival. So we-- we each went to a different way, like-- and in the morning, it was about 6 o'clock, the court reporter was living in a villa right across from the second ghetto, from the middle ghetto. And he looked out the window. He didn't see any Germans. So he said, you know, Dorka, you can go home, because I don't see any Germans. So, you know, I was 11 years old. He told me to go home. So I left all the merchandise that I carried to him. And I climbed the-- there was a smaller fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I walked where the division was from this middle ghetto to this our ghetto, there were-- there were railroads from the train and a road. So he-- I saw that trucks were coming with soldiers. So I took off my wooden shoes and I ran quick home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when my father and mother saw me, they said, what are you doing here? You're supposed to be with Ludwiczek. Why did you come home? So I told them, he told me to go home. And my father said, quick, run to Rachel. And I knew where the other one is. And the other one was at the end of our ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to the wall. And my mother ran with me, to be sure that I get across. And when I came, it was a chicken fence. And I was 11. I was a big girl. I couldn't climb it. But the Germans were already there with the guns. And one German that was close to where-- he was coming to where I was, he didn't know I'm Jewish, because my mother didn't come to me to help to cross. She didn't want to cause me any problems, because she wore this peyah, you know, the wig, so you could tell she's Jewish, because Polish people did not wear the wigs. The German soldier helped me across the fence, because it was moving back and forth and I couldn't make it. See, a wooden-- a wooden fence I could do. A block fence I could do. But this chicken fence, I couldn't. And he didn't know I'm Jewish. I had braids and, you know, the blue eyes. And he didn't think I'm Jewish. And the villa of this sergeant was-- was right next to this ghetto. He saw where I went in. And I came in there. It was daytime. And I told him I came with Rachel. And she was under the bed. And I-- he put me under the bed. And I told my sister what happened. At nighttime, the Germans started searching around the area with dogs, if there are any Jews. And the sergeant was afraid that they might come to the house. And if he comes with the dog, he'll find us under the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told us, he says, I'm very sorry. I can't keep you. You have to leave my house. You go to the outhouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the morning, you go to Parysow, to a city, where not far from that city is a farm. My cousin is there. So when I hear that that ghetto's going to be evacuated, I'll come and tell you. And we trusted him, because we knew him for a long time. And that's how it was. We stayed in the outhouse 'til curfew was over, like from let's say, 2:00 in the morning or maybe earlier. And we went one station forward so people shouldn't know us. And we took that train to-- it was a-- not an electric train, because the electric train came only from Warsaw to Otwock no more. So we had to take a train that goes slowly. In Polish. It's a [POLISH]. And we went there. And we went to the ghetto. And it happened that we had a distant cousin in there, so we stayed with them. And there was a river. There was no fence, no gates, nothing. But as I said, in my city, nobody dared to go out, because the Polish people were just like the Germans. They would turn you in if you would go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there. And sure enough, one day a few weeks later, this sergeant shouted across from this small river that you could almost jump over it, look up Rachel Kirszenbaum and tell her that I'm waiting for her. And word from mouth came. And we went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I forgot to tell you that in the outhouse, it looks like the bacteria got us, because both of us started to itch on the hands. And it was like bumps on them. And my sister got an infection. And she had to go to the hospital in that ghetto, in Parysow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was with that distant family. I didn't know them. And I was visiting my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when he came, we left again to go-- just the day before it was to be again taken away to Treblinka they also went to this farmer, not far from that city. And we stayed there a few days. And he again didn't want to keep us. He said-- we stayed in the barn upstairs there where they keep the straw. He said, sorry. I can't keep you anymore. So we went back home to Otwock. And on the train, people came up to my sister and said, what are you doing with that Jewish kid, on me, because I was very frightened. And it showed. My sister could-- she was older than me, so she could-- you saw the picture before that she pulled me. I was then five years old. And she was already--

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll show it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's 84, so she's also older. I'm the youngest. And as we went off the train in our city, where are we going? Nobody wants us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had a distant somebody she knew on the other side of the city. We went there. And we stayed in the cellar. And we're thinking, what can we do? And she said to me, Rachel, you know I can't be with you, because we both will die. So she said, you better go back to camp, where my sister and brother and brother-in-law are. The labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The labor camp. So I walked there, because it was a straight way where this train went, the slow-going train, so I walked with the train. And I went to camp. And they hid me for a few weeks. But every day and every night was like a year. The Germans were coming for inspections to be sure that no other Jew that doesn't belong there is coming or hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Karczew?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Karczew. And they came-- they wouldn't go near those beds, bunkbeds, because there were bed bugs. So my brother made a pillow out of me on the third one when they heard that they're coming. Sometimes, there was no warning. And the bed bugs were biting me, but I wasn't allowed to move. You see, if I move, I give myself away. And I wasn't that little. So it was really-- later on, I had bumps all over. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then they saw that they started coming with dogs. So the dogs would bark that somebody is there. So my brother-in-law said, well, you can't stay in the camp anymore. We'll give you to farmers nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was with one farmer. He kept me because I was knitting. Somehow, I don't know, God must've given me the wisdom to be able, without books or anything. I could make gloves and scarves. And I was really knitting very fast. I made once a dress for a little girl from little pieces with different colors. How I-- I couldn't do it now for sure. How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was still, I'm talking about 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was still just about--

speaker: interviewee
text: 12. I was there 19-- yeah, I was about 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. Yeah. I forgot when the ghetto of Warsaw was liquidated. That's when they liquidated Karczew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was '42. So you see what I mean, that every day and every night was like a year? It just stretches.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was with this farmer. My brother brought him something for me, for keeping me. And he took off the white star.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, the Polish people right away told the Germans. And here we sit. And I had my father's-- my parents' picture, that family picture with me. And we saw the Germans are coming. And here is my brother. And he was really-- you could tell he's Jewish. I took-- it was a Sunday. I'll never forget it. I took the picture out from where I hid it on me. And I put it under the tablecloth. And the Germans came in and we were sitting around the table. I was part of the farmers, you see, with braids. But my brother wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You Jew, what are you doing here? Where is your permission to go out of camp? Why don't you wear your white armband?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said that the-- the [GERMAN], that means the-- my brother-in-law was. He sent me here, to this farmer. So he took him by the neck and he says, let's see if you're telling the truth. And he took him to the camp. And sure enough, my brother-in-law stuck up for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that fizzed out again. And I went to another family to be in a one-room little house. And I stayed there just a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when the Warsaw ghetto we saw is burning, they knew that they are next. But my brother-in-law was lucky, because he had from before the war a mill, a wood-- that his brother was buying whole forests. And they would bring the wood there and make boards. And the farmers would come to buy for wood-- for sheds, for whatever they needed the wood. And there was one farmer that my brother-in-law got very friendly with.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marek. Marek Asch. And he-- this old man said to him, he said, if you ever need a place, I'm going to make for you a bunker. I'll pretend that I'm making running water. And I'll build a big bunker and put pipes. If somebody will come in, I'll put some water down to show that we are really using it and you'll be hiding there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew of that farmer too. Osuch was his name. And so he couldn't take me with to that bunker, because it was too small. My brother was there, my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the bunker that the farmer was building by the mill and that it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The farmer was building in his farmland--

speaker: interviewer
text: In his farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --near his farm. But he knew my brother-in-law from the mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was an old guy, married a second time. He had two sets of kids. But a very nice Polish fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: My brother-in-law wanted to sign the mill over to him and he wouldn't take it. Can you imagine? Such a nice fellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew where they are. And my sister said, if you ever need us, but be very careful, you can come and see us. And they gave me to another farmer that they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: They who?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, my brother-in-law. And I went to that farmer as they were emptying the-- the camp. You see, they were rounding up the people. And I heard a lot of shooting. And I was on the attic. And I was crying, because I didn't know if my sisters had-- my sister and her husband and my brother had time to run away. So I cried most of the night because I heard the shootings. And then I fell asleep towards the morning. And she comes up and she says, this farmer, she said, you can't stay in the house, because the Germans are coming with dogs and they want to see if somebody escaped. So I thought that I am in a cellar. And I fell down from the attic, because I wanted to go up in the hole. And I fell down. And it looks like I have the deviated septum from it. And she said, go in the fields and-- and I'll bring you lunch. So I went very carefully in between the-- the wheat or whatever was growing, not to make a path so they see. And I laid down there and I was sleeping. In comes a dog with a ranger, the ones that look if the forest is on fire. They call it the ranger. And I was so scared, the German Shepherd. And he held him very tight. And he said, you must be a Jew. I said, no, I'm not. He said, don't tell me you're not Jewish. Polish children do not sleep like that. And he took me by the neck, and he says, give me money. I know all Jews have money. I said, I don't have any money. I really didn't. He says, then I'll take you to the Germans. And he took me by the neck. And he guides me out to the path. And then this old lady, the farmer, is coming with lunch for me. And they-- apparently she knew his parents. And she knew him since he was born. His parents were not alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, shame on you. What kind of Christian are you? You're going to church every week to pray? What do you want with that child? She didn't say, 'Jewish child,' just 'child.' And he got very embarrassed from this-- she was white-haired, little farmer. And he went away. And she said to me, you know what? When night comes, you can't stay here anymore. You have to go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want to endanger my sister, Tofka, and my brother-in-law, and my brother, because I-- I knew I can't be there with them. So I-- when it was dark, I went to the forest. And I took off my wooden shoes again, not to make noise. And I was walking and crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, where am I going to go now? I don't know. But somehow, the instinct took me towards my city. And-- I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came upon a place that the earth moved. I didn't know what it was, but I quickly ran across. It was quite a big area. Years later, I found out that they killed a lot of Jews and buried them half-alive. And-- and I walked through it. It was moving. It wasn't my imagination.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started running after that. Where do I run? Where do I go? Nobody wants me. It was just, I tell you, the minutes were like years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when I finished going through the forest, it was like a way to go to Warsaw that they built when I was a little girl. We all went there to see it being-- how they built it. People used to go on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cycles, on bicycles to Warsaw. That's how-- it was a beautiful road.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remembered suddenly, like it came out from nowhere, there lives the woman that used to come to help my mother wash the laundry. And how do I remembered her? Because she was a widow with two kids. And my mother was always good to her. She says come on, Dorka, here is some stuff. Take over to-- what was her name? Pawlowa. Take-- You know that, for years, I couldn't remember her name? And-- and then one day a few weeks ago I sat up in the middle of the night. Pawlowa! It's like-- it was so funny.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was also a one-room house. And I knocked on the door towards morning, because you didn't dare go at night. They would shoot you. That was curfew. She knew me, of course. And she took me in, as poor as she was. She told her two children that went to school, don't you dare say that we keep Dorka here, because I'll kill you. And whoever-- she had a latch, not a key, that you latch door. And when somebody knocked on the door, because it was locked like this, she kept it open, I had to go under the bed, because they didn't even have a cupboard to go into.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I stayed there through the winter. After the winter, she said, you know what? I can't keep you anymore. Where am I going? I went to Karczew, where my sister and her husband and my brother were hidden. And I knew the farmers too. So I waited 'til the night, because you couldn't go at daytime. No neighbors should see you. I was in the garden 'til it got dark. And then I walked up to see nobody sees me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she opened the door. And I said, I have to see my sister. I'm not staying. But I just have to ask her what I should do next. I don't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she led me down. It was like a cellar. And he dug a hole to go down. And then there was another hole to go up. And he covered that part, so if somebody comes to the cellar, they don't see anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was a dark, really hole, that was like a half a-- a flat brick, like this one, the bigger one. That's all for the air. They weren't allowed to talk. They weren't allowed to burn a candle. They had just to be there quiet. It was very small and low. My brother was then about six feet tall, maybe a little lower. He couldn't get up. He just had to walk like this. There wasn't much to walk. It was just a little bed here, a little bed here, and a little table in the middle. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to my sister, after I came in, my God, what am I going to do? Nobody wants me. So she said, you know what? You go to Maliszewska. She was-- the church on our street.

speaker: interviewee
text: We also were friends with her, through my father. As I said, he was very respected. He always walked out very clean. And he insisted on us having always very shiny shoes and clean clothes. And like nobody should say dirty Jew. So to this day, I got that from my father, that you have to be neat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went there. And I said, what am I going to do? She said, go to this Maliszewska and ask her for a birth certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after you get the birth certificate, go to Warsaw. She gave me some money for the train and some money for the newspaper. It was pennies that time, wasn't very expensive. And get to a mother's helper. Whoever needs a mother's helper, you go and get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when morning came, I snuck out again. And I walked-- didn't want to use up the money-- I walked again to our city, from Karczew to Otwock, where the train was going. And of course, I went to my street. And I came towards the evening, just before curfew, to this Maliszewska. What are you doing here? And I told her that I need her to do me a favor, to give me a birth certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, sorry, I cannot give it to you. But I'll give you an idea of where to get it. You go with the electric train one station over Warsaw, the other side of Warsaw, the first station after Warsaw. I forgot the name. It was with a P. And you go there to the church's registrar. But you must go first to the cemetery and find a child that died when you were more or less born so you can have more or less the same age. And that's was it. But at night, she wouldn't keep me in the house and she had a barn. And in the barn was a goat. It was cold already that time. And if we wouldn't have a goat, I'd be afraid to go near it. But I put my back against the goat and the goat warmed me. And I wasn't afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So toward the morning, I went and I took the train to Warsaw, one station above. And I went to the-- I asked the people where the cemetery is. And I come before the cemetery. And I almost died.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was maneuvers of the Germans, with dogs and guns and tanks. I said, oh my God. My heart was pounding. All they had to do is put their hand on my heart. That would give it away. But I went with a high head and pretended I'm not scared. And they actually didn't pay attention that much for Polish children, only if a German pointed-- a Polish pointed the finger. And I walked around the cemetery. Sure enough, I found a child that was born and died the same day. So I had to brainwash myself-- I didn't have a pencil and paper-- the name of the child, the date-- the month that was born, and the year. So I walked around and walked back and walked around again, until I brainwashed myself. I knew for sure that this I'll remember. I went and asked for where the church is. And I came in. There were two older women. I shouldn't say old, because now I'm getting old. They had white hair. And I told them, I said I need a birth certificate. Somebody asked me to get them. I am from Lublin. And they looked at each other. They said, I can't give it to you. My husband isn't here. I said, but my train is going to go away. And I promised the people. You see for me, I will start at home. A promise is holy. I promised the people that I would get it. And the train will go away and I don't have where to be. I have to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they again looked at each other, and they think, oh, well. It was like either 50 cents or a zloty, that time, the birth certificate. And they finally gave it to me. And I took it. And now that I had the birth certificate and a new name-- What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had an idea, Zofia Lesczinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was Zofia born? What was the birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was March, the 8th, 1931.
